channel.title: physics.ed-ph updates on arXiv.org
channel.description: physics.ed-ph updates on the arXiv.org e-print archive.

item.title: Introducing Artificial Neural Networks in the Physics Laboratory: A Compound Pendulum Case Study
item.description: arXiv:2503.23506v3 Announce Type: replace Abstract: Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) have emerged as a powerful tool in physics research by enabling data-driven modeling, improving analysis of complex and noisy systems, and complementing traditional theoretical approaches. From a pedagogical perspective, incorporating ANNs into physics teaching lab-oratories helps students effectively bridge theory, experiment, and modern computational techniques. In this study, we introduce ANN implementation into the undergraduate physics laboratory, utilizing a traditional compound pendulum experiment as a case study for determining the acceleration due to gravity, g. The intention is not to replace the traditional experimental method but to extend it by using ANN as a complementary tool for analyzing and validating experimental data. Students first measure parameters such as effective length, time period, and initial angular displacement, then compute g using standard analytical methods along with error estimation. These experimentally obtained parameters serve as in-puts to the ANN model, which consists of input, hidden, and output layers that predict g. The dataset is divided into training (70%) and validation/testing (30%) subsets. Using the training data, the model predicts g, calculates error by comparing with actual results, and optimizes itself further. Once trained, the ANN makes predictions for the test dataset. Through the traditional experiment, we estimate g as 1009.03 +/- 6.82 cm/s2, while the ANN model yields 1009.029858 +/- 0.000592 cm/s2. Both methods produce nearly identical values, confirming the validity of the experiment. However, the ANN estimate carries a significantly smaller uncertainty, indicating much higher precision and consistency in data handling. This suggests that while the conventional approach remains reliable, ANN is more effective in minimizing the impact of experimental noise and errors.
item.dc:creator: Saralasrita Mohanty, Prabhu Prasad Tripathy, Raja Das, Sudakshina Prusty
